title: Ferroelectric brightening of spin forbidden dark excitons in a WSe2/hybrid perovskite heterostructure
authors: Xinyun Wang, Magdalena Grzeszczyk, Maxim Trushin, Ivan Verzhbitskiy, Dmitrii Litvinov, Yi Wei Ho, Yuan Chen, Zhenyue Wu, Mykola Telychko, Chuanqi Zhang, Andres Granados del Aguila, Kuan Eng Johnson Goh, Xinwei Li, Goki Eda, Shaffique Adam, Maciej Koperski, Kian Ping Loh
abstract: Long-lived dark excitons in monolayer WSe2 present promising candidates for carrying spin and valley information, but their optical access and spin manipulation have conventionally required the use of strong external magnetic fields. Here, using a ferroelectric hybrid perovskite heterostructure, we leverage the ferroelectric proximity effect to break the WSe2's in-plane rotational symmetry and brighten the spin-forbidden dark excitons under zero magnetic field conditions. Furthermore, we show that the twist angle between the WSe2 and perovskite crystals controls the ferroelectric coupling strength and valley-contrasting polarization. Our proposed mechanism, supported by a four-band tight-binding model, suggests that the ferroelectric proximity effect induces an asymmetric intersublattice interaction, generating an effective in-plane spin-orbit coupling (SOC) field that rotates spin/valley polarization and brightens dark excitons. Our work establishes ferroelectric proximity coupling as an electrically reconfigurable, magnetic-field-free strategy for spin exciton control in two-dimensional semiconductors.
summary: The research demonstrates that interfacing monolayer $	ext{WSe}_2$ with a ferroelectric perovskite substrate allows for the electrical activation of spin-forbidden dark excitons. By leveraging the ferroelectric proximity effect, the system generates an effective in-plane SOC field, enabling magnetic-field-free control over exciton spin and valley polarization. This establishes a powerful, electrically tunable platform for quantum information processing in 2D materials.
main_problem: The primary goal is achieving electrical reconfigurable, magnetic-field-free control over spin and valley information carried by long-lived dark excitons in 2D semiconductors.
main_result: The ferroelectric proximity effect successfully brightens the spin-forbidden dark exciton ($	ext{X}_{	ext{D}}$) in $	ext{WSe}_2$ at zero magnetic field, establishing a symmetry-tunable control mechanism.
method: The study combines experimental characterization (PL, SHG, Raman) with theoretical modeling using a four-band tight-binding Hamiltonian to explain the underlying physics.
model_or_system: The system is a $	ext{WSe}_2$/ferroelectric hybrid perovskite heterostructure. The mechanism relies on the ferroelectric proximity effect inducing an asymmetric intersublattice interaction, which generates an effective in-plane spin-orbit coupling (SOC) field.
key_observables: Brightening of dark excitons at zero magnetic field; polarization rotation/modulation proportional to the electric field orientation; reduction in exciton polarization phase difference indicating valley mixing.
important_parameters: Ferroelectric field ($\mathbf{E}$); interlayer twist angle; applied magnetic fields ($B_{//}, B_{\perp}$).
main_assumptions: The mechanism is modeled by assuming the ferroelectric proximity effect dictates an asymmetric hopping term in the tight-binding Hamiltonian.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate symmetry breaking via SHG polar maps, and PL spectra confirm new excitonic peaks emerging at zero magnetic field with characteristic magneto-optical responses.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical platform ($	ext{WSe}_2$/perovskite), presents experimental evidence of exciton brightening (PL/SHG), develops a theoretical model based on symmetry breaking and SOC, and finally demonstrates control via electric field tuning and twist angle dependence.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses open quantum systems by demonstrating how an external, tunable electric field can engineer spin-orbit coupling to activate otherwise optically forbidden transitions in solid-state excitons, which is highly relevant for building quantum light sources or qubits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
